record_office_terminology.land_records: Registro de la Propiedad (Property Registry, established under Spanish Ley Hipotecaria 1870)
record_office_terminology.probate: Tribunal de Primera Instancia (Court of First Instance, formerly under Spanish colonial courts)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Registro Demográfico (civil registry from 1885 under Spain; continued under US administration)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1885 (civil registration under Spain by Royal Decree; US administration continued system after 1898)

burned_county_authority: No formal 'burned county' designation exists for Puerto Rico. Record losses are primarily from hurricanes (notably San Ciriaco 1899, San Felipe II 1928, Hugo 1989, Georges 1998, María 2017), tropical climate deterioration, and the 1918 Mayagüez earthquake. The Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR) in San Juan is the central repository for surviving colonial and territorial records.
administrative_notes: Puerto Rico has been a US territory since the 1898 Spanish-American War (Treaty of Paris). Previously a Spanish colony from 1508 (Caparra settlement). The island is divided into 78 municipios, each functioning as a county-equivalent jurisdiction. Catholic parish records are the primary genealogical source, predating civil registration (1885) by centuries. Spanish naming conventions apply — double surnames (paterno materno), with the father's surname listed first. Slavery was abolished in 1873 under Spanish law (Ley Moret 1870, abolition decree 1873). Notarial protocols (protocolos notariales) under Spanish legal tradition are unique and essential records. The Jones Act of 1917 granted US citizenship to Puerto Ricans. Operation Bootstrap (1947-1960s) caused massive migration to the US mainland.

core_repositories.repository: Instituto de Cultura Puertorriqueña, San Juan
core_repositories.description: Puerto Rico's central archival repository, housed within the Instituto de Cultura Puertorriqueña in Old San Juan. Holdings include notarial protocols (protocolos notariales) from the 16th century, Spanish colonial government records, municipal records, military records, slave registers, civil registry duplicates, census records (padrones), and land records. Essential for all Puerto Rico genealogical research.

core_repositories.repository: Universidad de Puerto Rico, Río Piedras
core_repositories.description: Research center at UPR Río Piedras with extensive manuscript collections, microfilm of Spanish colonial records, parish registers, and historical documentation. The CIH holds copies of records from the Archivo General de Indias (Seville) relevant to Puerto Rico.

core_repositories.source_name: Colección Puertorriqueña — Biblioteca José M. Lázaro
core_repositories.repository: Universidad de Puerto Rico, Río Piedras
core_repositories.description: The most comprehensive collection of Puerto Rican printed materials, including newspapers, genealogies, local histories, city directories, and government publications. Houses rare books, maps, and photographs essential for historical context.

core_repositories.repository: Fundación Luis Muñoz Marín, Trujillo Alto
core_repositories.description: Archives of Puerto Rico's first elected governor. Holdings include personal papers, government correspondence, photographs, and documentation of Operation Bootstrap, the Commonwealth (Estado Libre Asociado) movement, and 20th-century political and social history.

core_repositories.repository: NARA, College Park, MD / Washington, DC
core_repositories.description: US federal records relating to Puerto Rico from 1898 forward. Record Group 186 (Spanish Governors of Puerto Rico), RG 350 (Bureau of Insular Affairs), military installation records, census schedules (1910-1950), and Selective Service records. Also holds records of the former US District Court for Puerto Rico.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive microfilmed and digitized collections including Catholic parish registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) from dozens of parishes, civil registry records, census records, and notarial protocols. FamilySearch microfilmed most Puerto Rico parish registers in partnership with the Catholic Church. Browsable and indexed image collections.
Comprehensive research guide with municipio-specific pages, record availability, parish information, and links to digitized microfilm collections. Essential starting point for Puerto Rico research.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Puerto Rico newspapers including La Correspondencia, El Mundo, La Democracia, and others. Coverage primarily 19th-20th century. Newspapers publish marriage, birth, and death announcements, legal notices, estate settlements, and community news in Spanish.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Universidad de Puerto Rico, Río Piedras
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: The most extensive collection of Puerto Rico newspapers, including La Gaceta de Puerto Rico (official gazette, 1806-1902), La Correspondencia de Puerto Rico, El Mundo, and hundreds of local and regional newspapers. Microfilm and some digitized access.

military_records.repository: AGPR, San Juan / Archivo General Militar de Segovia, Spain
military_records.description: Spanish colonial militia rolls (padrones de milicias), officer service records, military census records, and garrison documentation from the 16th-19th centuries. Puerto Rico's strategic military importance generated extensive records. Includes pardo (mixed-race) and moreno (Black) militia records, essential for African-descended genealogy.

military_records.source_name: NARA — US Military Records for Puerto Rico
military_records.description: US military records from 1898: Spanish-American War records, WWI and WWII service records (65th Infantry Regiment — Borinqueneers), Korean War, Selective Service records, and installation records for Forts Buchanan, Allen, Brooke, Camp Santiago, Roosevelt Roads, and Ramey AFB.

land_and_property.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
land_and_property.description: Notarial protocols are the backbone of Puerto Rico land and legal records under Spanish civil law tradition. Notaries recorded property sales, testaments (wills), dowry agreements, slave transactions, coartación (self-purchase) contracts, powers of attorney, and partnership agreements. Protocols from the 16th century survive at AGPR. Each notarial district maintained separate series.

Formal property registration under the Spanish Ley Hipotecaria system (1870-present). Creates chains of title documenting property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and encumbrances. Regional property registry offices serve multiple municipios. Records are public and searchable.

vital_records.description: Central office for vital records (births, deaths, marriages) from 1885 to present. Civil registration began under Spanish law by Royal Decree in 1885. Duplicate copies of local registrations were sent to the central office. For pre-1885 vital events, Catholic parish registers are the primary source.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Archdiocese of San Juan / Cathedral of San Juan Bautista
church_and_cemetery.description: Central archives for the Catholic Church in Puerto Rico. Holds parish registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) from the oldest parishes dating to the 1500s-1600s. The Cathedral of San Juan Bautista has the island's oldest surviving registers. Diocesan records include marriage dispensations (dispensas), confirmations, and ecclesiastical census records.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Find A Grave — Puerto Rico Cemeteries
church_and_cemetery.description: Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Puerto Rico. Municipal cemeteries (cementerios municipales) were established in most municipios during the 19th century. Coverage varies by municipio. Catholic church cemeteries predate municipal cemeteries.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Puerto Rican genealogical society. Publishes genealogical research, hosts conferences, and maintains indexes to Puerto Rico records. Members contribute to family history research across all 78 municipios.
Volunteer-contributed municipio-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, church record extracts, historical sketches, and research guides for all 78 Puerto Rico municipios.
Authoritative reference for Puerto Rico history, culture, and geography. Includes municipio histories, biographical entries, and historical context essential for genealogical research planning.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Puerto Rico does not have extinct or abolished municipios in the traditional sense. However, jurisdictional boundaries have shifted significantly since the Spanish colonial period. The 78 current municipios evolved from as few as 5 original partidos (San Juan, Ponce, San Germán, Aguada, Coamo/Arecibo) established in the 16th-17th centuries. Florida (1971) is the most recently created municipio, carved from Barceloneta, Arecibo, and Manatí. Barrio boundaries within municipios have also changed over time — researchers should consult historical maps at AGPR and the US Census Bureau's Geographic Area Reference Files for boundary changes.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Slave registers mandated by Spanish colonial authorities, particularly the comprehensive 1872 census of enslaved persons conducted before the 1873 Abolition Law. Registers list enslaved individuals by name, age, origin, occupation, and slaveholder. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, manumission records, and liberto (freed persons) documentation. Essential for tracing African-descended families in Puerto Rico.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Church Records of Enslaved and Free Black Persons
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Catholic parish registers recorded baptisms, marriages, and burials of enslaved and free Black (negro, pardo, moreno) persons in separate or designated sections. These records often provide the only documentation of enslaved families, including godparent (padrino/madrina) relationships that indicate social networks. FamilySearch microfilm includes these registers.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Notarial Protocols — Slave Sales and Coartación Records
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Notarial protocols document slave sales, coartación (self-purchase) contracts, manumission deeds, and estate inventories listing enslaved persons. Coartación was a legal mechanism under Spanish law allowing enslaved individuals to negotiate their freedom through installment payments — these contracts provide detailed personal information. Slavery was abolished in Puerto Rico on March 22, 1873.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: AGPR, San Juan / Archivo General de Indias, Seville
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Immigration records for Spanish colonists arriving in Puerto Rico, including passenger lists, licencias de embarque (embarkation permits), and letters of recommendation. The 1815 Real Cédula de Gracias opened Puerto Rico to non-Spanish immigration, attracting Corsican, French, Irish, German, Venezuelan, and other settlers. AGPR holds cedulas de vecindad (residency certificates) and naturalization records.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: NARA — Puerto Rico Passenger and Immigration Records (post-1898)
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Post-1898 immigration and passenger records for Puerto Rico under US administration. Includes passenger arrival manifests, immigrant visa records, and naturalization records from the US District Court for Puerto Rico. Also documents migration between Puerto Rico and the US mainland, particularly during the Great Migration (1940s-1960s).

burned_counties_summary.notes: Puerto Rico does not have a 'burned county' designation. Record losses are primarily from natural disasters (hurricanes, earthquakes) and tropical climate deterioration. Hurricane María (2017) caused widespread damage to municipal infrastructure, particularly in mountain municipios like Utuado, Comerío, Jayuya, and Yabucoa. The 1918 earthquake damaged records in Mayagüez and western municipios. The Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR) serves as the central repository and has preserved most colonial-era records in climate-controlled storage.
